ontology.foodex2_description: Chrysanthemum greens (Glebionis coronaria) — boiled leafy vegetable
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for garland chrysanthemum as a food item; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side-dish serving of boiled Tang Ho is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small bowl of blanched leafy greens as served in Singaporean home cooking or hawker-style meals.

nutrient_source.title: Boiled garland chrysanthemum (Tang ho) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for boiled garland chrysanthemum (Tang ho), CR ID F14010009. All per-100g values are as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for boiled leafy green vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for boiled garland chrysanthemum (Tang ho) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous boiled non-starchy leafy green vegetables (e.g., boiled spinach, boiled Chinese cabbage) which consistently register GI ≤ 15 in the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 4.31 g/100g; GL per 100g serving = (15 × 4.31) / 100 = 0.65, rounded to 0.6. This is consistent with the very low GI category for non-starchy leafy greens.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally high potassium-to-sodium ratio (569 mg K : 53 mg Na per 100g)
hypertension.guidance: Boiled Tang Ho is highly suitable for hypertension management; its potassium content of 569 mg/100g combined with low sodium of 53 mg/100g provides one of the most favourable potassium-to-sodium ratios among leafy vegetables, directly supporting blood pressure reduction in line with DASH dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (4.31 g/100g), low sugar (2.01 g/100g), and estimated GI of 15
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled Tang Ho is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management; with only 4.31 g carbohydrate and 2.01 g sugar per 100g, an estimated GI of 15, and a glycaemic load of 0.6 per serving, it produces minimal postprandial glucose excursion and can be consumed freely within a carbohydrate-controlled meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat (0.09 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high potassium, and significant dietary fibre (2.3 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled Tang Ho is cardioprotective; its negligible total fat, confirmed zero cholesterol, high potassium (569 mg/100g) supporting vascular tone, and 2.3 g dietary fibre per 100g contributing to LDL reduction make it an excellent choice for cardiovascular disease prevention and management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of direct anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols) despite micronutrient richness
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled Tang Ho provides useful micronutrients (calcium 69 mg, iron 3.74 mg, potassium 569 mg per 100g) that support bone health in RA patients on corticosteroids, but it lacks direct anti-inflammatory factors such as omega-3 fatty acids or polyphenols; it is nutritionally neutral for RA and should be combined with omega-3-rich foods for anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled garland chrysanthemum, known in Singapore and across Chinese-speaking communities as Tang Ho (茼蒿), is a tender leafy green vegetable produced by blanching or boiling the shoots and leaves of Glebionis coronaria (formerly Chrysanthemum coronarium). The cooked vegetable has a soft, slightly wilted texture with a mild, pleasantly bitter and aromatic flavour characteristic of the chrysanthemum family. It is widely consumed in Singapore, Hong Kong, Taiwan, and mainland China as a side dish, hot-pot ingredient, or accompaniment to rice and noodle meals. Nutritionally, boiled Tang Ho is a low-calorie food at only 20 kcal per 100g, with 1.64 g protein, 0.09 g fat, and 4.31 g carbohydrate. It is exceptionally rich in potassium at 569 mg/100g — among the highest of any commonly consumed leafy vegetable — and provides 69 mg calcium and 3.74 mg iron per 100g, making it valuable for bone health and iron status. Dietary fibre is 2.3 g/100g. Sodium is low at 53 mg/100g. The estimated glycaemic index is 15 (low), with a glycaemic load of 0.6 per 100g serving. Clinically, boiled Tang Ho is highly suitable for hypertension (outstanding potassium-to-sodium ratio supporting DASH principles), Type 2 Diabetes (very low GI and carbohydrate load), and cardiovascular disease prevention (negligible fat, zero cholesterol, fibre contribution). It is fully vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; boiled garland chrysanthemum contains negligible saturated fat). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero; plant-based leafy vegetable with no added fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; plant-based food naturally contains no cholesterol). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for boiled garland chrysanthemum (F14010009) as of the accessed date. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been retained in dietary_flags context; with 4.31 g carbohydrate per 100g it is borderline keto-compatible in small portions. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on boiled non-starchy leafy greens in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for Tang Ho was located.
